title: Sums of certain arithmetic functions over 𝔽q[T] and non-unitary distributions
speaker: Matilde Lalin
abstract: In 2018 Keating, Rodgers, Roditty-Gershon and Rudnick established relationships of the mean-square of sums of the divisor function $d_k(f)$ over short intervals and over arithmetic progressions for the function field $\mathbb{F}_q[T]$ to certain integrals over the ensemble of unitary matrices when $q \rightarrow \infty$. We study similar problems leading to integrals over the ensembles of symplectic and orthogonal matrices when $q \rightarrow \infty$.  This is joint work with Vivian Kuperberg.
tags: 50 Years of Number Theory and Random Matrix Theory Conference

proof_stages.label: Classical divisor-sum variance templates
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the k-divisor function over the integers, recalls divisor-sum error terms, and states the Keating–Rodgers–Roditty-Gershon–Rudnick variance predictions for short intervals and arithmetic progressions in terms of a piecewise-polynomial gamma_k.

proof_stages.label: Function-field unitary model for KR3
proof_stages.summary: The integer problems are translated to F_q[T], where q-to-infinity variance formulas become unitary matrix integrals whose leading asymptotics are lattice-point counts and yield the same gamma_k(c).

proof_stages.label: Symplectic square-class problem
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by replacing unitary distributions by symplectic ones, the speaker formulates the prime-conductor problem of summing d_k(f) over polynomials f that are squares modulo a prime p and states the resulting variance as an integral over Sp(2g).

proof_stages.label: Quadratic-character and Frobenius reduction
proof_stages.summary: Quadratic characters and hyperelliptic L-functions convert the square-detection variance into sums of secular coefficients of symplectic Frobenius classes, and Katz equidistribution for prime or fixed-factorization conductors supplies the limiting symplectic matrix integral.

proof_stages.label: Schur-function encoding of the symplectic integral
proof_stages.summary: To analyze the symplectic integral, the talk introduces partitions, semistandard Young tableaux, Schur functions, and a Bump–Gamburd formula, then specializes variables to a two-variable generating function whose diagonal coefficient retains the variance square.

proof_stages.label: Polytope and determinant analysis of gamma
proof_stages.summary: The tableaux are parameterized by rightmost-entry variables to count lattice points in a constrained polytope, giving the predicted degree 2k^2+k-2, and a determinantal formula is used to compute partial zero-sector formulas.

proof_stages.label: Number-field conjectural transfer
proof_stages.summary: The function-field symplectic calculation is used to propose an integer-number-field variance conjecture for divisor sums over numbers that are quadratic residues modulo primes p in a dyadic range.

proof_stages.label: Sector analogues, orthogonal variants, and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The talk presents a sector-condition analogue related to Gaussian-prime arguments, notes a Katz twist leading toward orthogonal distributions, and lists open problems on matrix-integral descriptions, lower terms, orthogonal degrees, convolutions, and higher correlations.

examples.name: Small-k piecewise-polynomial gamma_k
examples.note: A low-k display of the KR3 function gamma_k(c) shows concretely how the leading variance coefficient is piecewise polynomial with transition points, but it only illustrates the already stated unitary pattern.
examples.engagement_reason: the small-k piecewise-polynomial gamma_k is only pointed to as a simple example with pieces, without deriving or computing the pieces in the transcript

passes.reason: a small-k instance of the standard gamma_k piecewise-polynomial family used illustratively, not as a failure case.
It is a first small-k two-piece instance of the general gamma_k piecewise-polynomial family, used illustratively.
The small-k γ_k is a nontrivial piecewise-polynomial member whose defining integral has strong permutation/reflection symmetry, and it is used illustratively.
A small-k instance of the piecewise-polynomial gamma_k is used only as an illustrative formula, with no extremal or sporadic structure singled out.

examples.name: Semistandard Young tableau of shape (6,4,2,2)
examples.note: The filled Young diagram is used to make the definitions of shape, semistandardness, type, Schur functions, and even partitions concrete before applying them to the matrix integral.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the tableau of shape (6,4,2,2) to identify row lengths, check semistandard row/column conditions, and count entries such as a_1 and a_2

passes.reason: an ordinary semistandard Young tableau with an even row-length shape used to illustrate the definition.

examples.name: Low-k zero-sector computations
examples.note: The k=1 and k=2 evaluations of the zero-subterm formula illustrate what the determinant method produces and how quickly the piecewise formulas become complicated.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the zero-sector determinant formula in low-k cases, saying it can be evaluated for k=1 and that they computed k=2

passes.reason: the low-k zero-sector formulas are base/truncated calculations in an edge sector of the parameter range.

examples.name: Arguments of Gaussian primes
examples.note: The classical question about angular distribution of Gaussian primes is presented as the source of the function-field sector problem, motivating why a sector condition is an analogue of short intervals.
examples.engagement_reason: arguments of Gaussian primes are cited as the motivating Rudnick-Waxman problem before moving to the function-field model, with no computation on Gaussian primes themselves

passes.reason: Gaussian-prime arguments are a standard motivating distribution problem, not a sporadic or adversarial example.
